Observations about my first psilocybin trip a couple days ago
Took around 1g of a strain called "penis envy". Interesting name! But supposedly one of the stronger variants.

Took around an hour before I felt anything.

When it started, I noticed that I didn't really feel much different mood-wise. Not more relaxed or more anxious, but just more... introspective?
I've been learning about the mind propositionally, through the theories of Lacanian psychoanalysis, Kegan's constructive-developmentalism, and Vervaeke's meaning crisis.
It felt like I had this buffer of propositional knowledge that I could finally process and realize in a participatory, perspectival way.

Like I finally GOT why (certain) people behave the way they do. I FELT the reality of our collective existence as conscious beings evolved from apes. Apes are graspers! We're graspers! It's what we do. WE GRASP
As Vervaeke said, we live in a state of modal confusion. Confusing the having mode with the being mode. Under psilocybin, I felt that boundary, that defence mechanism, start to collapse.

As humans, we almost REQUIRE that we are blissfully unaware of the reality of our existence. That we are just apes, that we are subject to, and a result of, the laws of nature and physics. I felt myself becoming more aware of this.
Maybe this is like Lacan's Real: it's the aspects of our reality that we are blind to _by necessity_, because we would otherwise not survive that level of raw awareness.

Besides a cascade of insights like this, there were also the visual artifacts that the drug is known for. Watching my ceiling, it appeared like waves moving slightly, but more importantly, it was like I was projecting a regular pattern on the surface which wasn't truly there.
Like my mind was trying to draw patterns where they didn't necessarily exist, seeing lines and repetition as you might see when you look out at the surface of the ocean.
Near the beginning of this experience, when I closed my eyes, there was some sort of glowing entity I felt.

Glowing in a visual sense, but it wasn't a strong visual, it was more of a felt presence. In a similar way that you can feel the presence of someone next to you in the dark, even though you can't see them

This entity felt mysterious and powerful, like it knows something. Like it has something that it's offering me, that I might want, but I initially momentarily pushed it away. It had a faint glow, but also had tendril-like artifacts, like it was growing and reaching towards me.
It kind of felt similar to a growing, spreading entity like you see in media like BotW's Malice, the blight in Horizon, or the cordyceps in The Last of Us.

I decided to open myself to it and let it come towards me, and it felt like I was consumed by it. Like I sort of became one with it. And nothing bad happened after that, which was a curiosity and a bit of a relief.

In my mind, this presence, this entity aligns with Agape, or maybe Eros. The love that most infants experience when they re-attach to their mother after birth, where one moves from Detachment to Psychosis. Where one "seals up" when moving from Kegan's stage 1 to stage 2 balance

Or the Big Other in a Lacanian sense: the entity could represent that. Where I was previously detached from the Big Other, it somehow revealed itself to me and I became one with it. I feel like it's still there, in a weaker sense, even days later.

I felt so much more ALIVE during this experience. I was literally walking around my apartment, moving my body in a way that felt so natural and... embodied. I felt a powerful, deep connection to reality, like when Neo in the Matrix realizes his control over his world.

The phrase I kept repeating as I was moving around was "I GET IT". And "FFUUUCK", in the sense of "Goddamn it how did it take me THIS LONG to realize basic principles of the project of human existence".
Overall, pretty solid experience. I feel more in control of myself, and more connected to my reality.

But I get now how psychedelic experiences can also lead you down paths of parasitic processing. You need to have the skills and context to be able to keep yourself on track, to avoid losing touch with reality. I feel confident that I did alright.
I notice that I feel more comfortable just talking to people now. Like I GET, to some degree, what they expect of me and how I can act accordingly. And I hope this carries forward.

I think there are two different styles of what people call "social anxiety". They differ by how they originate. Call them
(1) social fear
(2) social anxiety

Social fear (1) comes from a detachment from social norms and expectations. The fearful person doesn't have a good internal model of how people will respond to them, so they are constantly guarding themselves, expending energy to blend in, consciously mask themselves.

Social fear correlates with the "avoidant attachment style" of attachment theory, and with the Detached structure of neo-Lacanian theory.

Stage 1 or 2 of Kegan's theory, not sure.

The Detached person doesn't have a good model of themselves OR other people.

On the other hand, social anxiety (2) comes from an overattachment to social norms and expectation. Unlike the fearful person, the anxious person has a good Theory of Mind of how other people see them. They define themselves by how they think they are perceived.

It's as if they feel they are constantly being "quizzed" or "tested" by the expectations that they sense others have of them.

Correlates with "anxious attachment style", the Neurotic structure of (neo-)Lacanian theory, and stage 3 of Kegan's theory.
I feel like I've dealt with social fear most of my life. With fleeting periods where I've felt I overcame it, where I "got" what people around me expected of me. https://t.co/EiOWnXAVAq
But they never lasted more than a few months, due to how much my social environment shifted around me, moving schools often during adolescence, and a home life where I feared a parent.
Only just recently, I feel like I've remembered this mode of being where I "get" the social expectations of me. Hanging on as much as I can.

I've noticed myself doing this thing where I project modesty on someone I'm talking to by downplaying something they've achieved or worked for.

I'm not at all trying to diminish their self worth, but I think it comes from this place of: "I would want to be modest about my own accomplishments, so I assume they would too, therefore I'm trying to connect with them by participating in this shared modesty together"
It's like I'm trying to get at this shared sense of collaboration, a level playing field of problem solving in which we can both uplift ourselves together, in a mutual, reciprocal manner.

I could be in a conversation with a coworker, solving a problem together, and I'll say "ah, this is hard. It's like we each have a part of the puzzle but there are still missing pieces. I think we both lack knowledge about X."
But actually, they know quite a bit about X, more than I do. It's not fair to them to have modesty projected onto them.

I want to get better at handling these scenarios. Keeping modesty for myself seems like the right path, while at the same time not overinflating the ego of the other party. Let them be the judge of the value of their own accomplishments.

i just went to an authentic relating meetup for the first time. some reflections:

i planned to go to the event with the intention to psychologically grow: specifically to practice attaching to people. i want to begin to feel intrinsically safe navigating the world of social interaction, to truly connect with people.

before the event, i was nervous and fearful that i might say or experience something that would negatively affect me.

it was both a fearful and hopeful feeling that i would be emotionally provoked by something. i wanted to feel something, to know that i can let my guard down.
upon arrival, i was somewhat relieved by the observation that the other attendees seemed equally nervous and apprehensive about the event. many of them were first-timers like me.

i briefly greeted someone who i recognized as a youtuber/twitter poster about attachment psychology!

i won't name them out respect for privacy, but i was mildly starstruck. i did not get a chance to connect with them but i wish i did.

i notice, and others noticed too, that i default to using mitigating language to describe my experiences. when asked how i feel or my opinion about something, i tend to say "i think x", raise my tone of voice, express a lack of confidence.
i expressed this in the check-in activity, which is where we went around the circle and introduced our names and 3 words for how we felt at that moment.

"my name is spencer and i am feeling nervous, cautious, but curious"

many others had similar, simple answers!

but yet others also had more "authentic" embodied responses. it's like they truly *feel* what they're saying; they're not afraid of how they might be judged.
what i didn't expect, and what i appreciate, is the framing provided by the group facilitator, that there is a difference between authenticity and transparency.
where transparency is raw, unfiltered openness and vulnerability, authenticity is about *externalizing your internal experience*, with discernment of the content being shared.
this event is not about being fully transparent, but about being authentic and present.
the next activity was to mingle with others in the room and present yourself as you would typically meet someone new, along with explaining with the *why* of why you put that self forward.

during this activity, i felt somewhat overwhelmed!

my response here was to explain how i present a calm, controlled, collected outward appearance. but on the inside, it's chaos, it's fear of the world and others, a lack of a felt sense of safety.
even the way i just framed this as a "response" rather than a participation, a relating, shows that i see myself reacting and adapting to the environment i find myself in

with one person i met as part of this activity, i felt a deeper resonance with him because he described his experience in a way that's similar to mine. a calm, controlled, slow outward appearance with a chaotic internal experience.
the next activity was to gather in groups of 3 and share a judgement we've made about another person in our lives, along with what they *should* do.

it was hard for me to find a specific event where i judged someone. but i found myself saying that i judge my mother as sad, or more aptly "pathetic" as suggested by a group member, and that she should get therapy and use shrooms (half jokingly).

the idea was to bring forth the judgemental shadow self that is normally suppressed. of all the activities today, this one was where i felt like i brought that self out most strongly, though it was still quite weak.
it's like the feeling of anger was deep inside and trying to emerge, but i still couldn't fully let my guard down.
another observation is that a few members commented on my appearance. i generally look quite young for my age, so they were surprised to learn i am 29.

the way this interaction goes is:

them: if you don't mind me asking, how old are you?
me: haha yeah, i get that a lot. i'm 29. i know i look young for my age, it's a whole identity thing that's been with me my whole life that i'm still figuring out how to deal with.
i don't really like the way i respond to that question though. what i actually want is to understand how my appearance affects their perception of me.

one member remarked that my initial entrance into the room brought the perception of someone who is introverted, shy, timid. this feels true to me.

but they were surprised to see me portray a more confident, self-assured self.

overall, i think it was a valuable experience (mitigating language again).

i have a desire to continue practicing things like this and return to this meetup, because i really want to increase my capacity for connection and introspection.
next time i might attend on a mild dose of psilocybin 🤔

i feel unsatisfied though, because even though i ostensibly related with people, i feel like i didn't truly connect with anyone enough to befriend them.

and it seemed like a lot of other ppl didn't either!
it was sort of a surreal experience. it's a totally different context than any normal social gathering, so perhaps the "making friends" aspect of this just doesn't register.
or even, the connection was *so fast and real* that this style of making friends by being our true selves is just unfamiliar territory. a new skill that i have only just started to learn.

trying to come to a model of how neo-lacanian development interacts with & constrains constructive-developmentalism (kegan)

the idea is that neo-lacanian structure is prior to kegan stage. it's more fundamental, and kegan stage is emergent from it.

your structure is a limit on your actual kegan stage, BUT i think you can temporarily "emulate" later stages from an earlier structure.

general correlations:

Detached : Impulsive (Kegan 1)
Psychotic : Imperial (Kegan 2)
Neurotic : Interpersonal (Kegan 3)
Perverted : Institutional (Kegan 4)
Seminal : Interindividual (Kegan 5)

i.e. a detached person is "naturally" at kegan stage 1 in terms of perception of self & other, affect. but it's possible to "emulate" the motivations, ethics of later kegan stages with conscious effort.

rationalists are mostly detached, but clearly they care about ethics

also, a child's structure can progress quite rapidly at a young age. you can get to perversion at age 3-4, but of course this doesn't mean you are at kegan 4. you still need to gain the experience & wisdom & skills necessary to progress in kegan stage

so with myself emerging from detachment, my "actual" kegan stage is 1-2, despite thinking of myself as 4. i try and do care about my relationships, and choosing my own principles & commitments, but in a sense it's faked/emulated.

i find it fascinating, the idea that the neo-lacanian structures can be applied to levels of social organization, not only inside one's psyche

you can categorize a company/community as a structure, because structure is just the mechanisms by which it develops & functions

detached organizations: nomadic, constantly shifting, changing course. 

like a startup or solo entrepreneur always pivoting, finding their footing.

or a music group that's always on tour

psychotic organizations: highly ritualized, cult-like qualities, confusion of reality and fantasy.

aligned towards The Purpose / The Mission, and hostile to disbelievers.

most early startups, political groups, circling / authentic relating, effective altruism groups

neurotic organizations: driven by social norms, following rules, conformity, doing "good" in a way that's constrained to what's acceptable. public image / appearances matter a lot.

probably most companies.

perverted organizations: recognize but reject social norms, a kind of "doing whatever they want" regardless of what is acceptable or expected. subversion of rules and authority.

punk rock movement, anonymous, burning man, rave culture, free software movement

seminal organizations, something like: actively participating & involved with driving a higher good in humanity. love, forgiveness, acceptance.

maybe: some forms of educational institutions, religious institutions, nonprofit charities

the eclipse punches a hole right through the regularity of it all.

there's nothing we can do to control it.

and nothing we can do to stop it.

as you progress through neo-lacanian structure, you gain bandwidth bc you learn new information processing mechanisms. you can more efficiently control of your awareness. https://t.co/WiP4kCCusB
if you're Detached, you're constantly Splitting. it's like you're always context-switching, bringing objects in and out of awareness. very expensive, energy-wise.

if you're Psychotic, you have Foreclosure. your awareness can remain expanded, you're not context-switching as much, and you can instead foreclose objects, selectively excluding things from awareness. more efficient.
if you're Neurotic, you can Repress objects to the unconscious, without needing to foreclose them. more integrated, more efficient.
if you're Perverse, you can Disavow objects. you acknowledge objects' existence, they remain in awareness, but you can actively choose not to be subject to them. more integrated, more efficient.

essentially, as you become more aware & accepting of objects in awareness, as you learn not to resist and split/foreclose/repress experiences, develop a more integrated psyche, your bandwidth usage is freed for higher-level tasks like empathy / theory of mind.

to start, going to paraphrase an example scenario described to be once by @TiffaniNWarren, demonstrating differences between ppl of diff structures:

let's say we have a bunch of ppl at a party.

Amy and Ben have a crush on each other, and everyone knows this in their own mind, but no one has said it explicitly. that is, everyone knows except the Big Other. the fact does not exist yet "in the external world".

if someone were to announce "Amy and Ben have a crush on each other!", then that's equivalent to telling the Big Other.

to most ppl, it would seem awkward and unacceptable to do that, but why?

it's because one "person" doesn't know: the Big Other. that's where meaning, social norms, and compulsion to act is "stored".

it doesn't change what ppl know, but it changes ppl's compulsion to act.

the early Detached person doesn't understand the Big Other, so they might make an explicit reference to it and make everyone feel embarrassed or awkward, and then become confused when everyone's mood suddenly changes.

the late Detached person usually knows better, but doesn't fully understand why - what's the big deal after all, if everyone already knows the fact of the crush?

but they can anticipate it would lead to an awkward mood.

Psychotics are different.

their relationship to the Big Other is akin to a mom-child relationship. there's a certain adversarial / rivalry / codependent quality to the relationship. imagine a typical toddler & how they act in anticipation of reward or punishment from mom.

in this case, their compulsion to act might depend on whether they think the Big Other would approve of/desire for Amy and Ben to be dating. so they now feel compelled to encourage them to hook up, cheer them on, etc. or otherwise discourage them, play it down, etc.

for the Psychotic, it's a compulsion; it feels like something they "have to" do, even if it conflicts with their other goals (maybe it makes their friendships with Amy and Ben awkward, maybe they had a crush on Amy themselves).

for the Neurotic, instead of what the Big Other desires in terms of Amy and Ben specifically, it's more about being a "good boy" or "good girl". it's following principles and rules.

is it more important to respect ppl's privacy? to promote love & match ppl with their soulmates?

would a good boy pull the Detached guy aside and admonish him for saying it out loud and not "following the rules"?

for the Neurotic, it can feel like being "graded" or judged by the Big Other on how well they handled the situation.

for the Pervert, it represents some material, an opportunity.

how can it be spun so that the Big Other comes away from it better off?

they might segue into a joke about how champagne makes all of us feel a little bit in love with each other, so that people are amused and feel relieved that the compulsion to act is removed from them.

the Pervert has more awareness of how others (Detached, Psychotic, Neurotic) are modelling the situation and their relationship to the Big Other, and so they understand how to create value and release tension from the situation.

compelled to "entertain" the Big Other.

for Seminals and later, the Big Other is dead.

there's no more direct compulsion to act, except they might in its memory or its honour.

so.
how's your relationship to the Big Other?

the newest term i've been obsessed with lately is "angloboxed". it's so good.

the way i understand it is it refers to the entity-ness that's implicit in the english language. the fact that everything has to be reified as a "thing", a concept, an identity. there's a permanence to every statement.

like "i'm a swimmer" or even just "i swim". somehow the language ties up your identity with the activity of swimming. it's nonspecific about time such that it seems like you've always been and always will be a swimmer.

to get less entity-oriented and more process-oriented in english, you can use -ing like "i'm swimming", to alleviate the permanence. but then the distinction is lost between swimming *at this moment* and swimming as a lifestyle/practice.

i'm doing swimming?

i'm living by virtue of swimming?

"angloboxed" is itself angloboxed.

you're not angloboxed as a rigid identity, you're just currently living by virtue of angloboxing.
i suspect that angloboxing is a major reason why ppl in the anglosphere get overly attached to identities.
"i'm a morning person" is angloboxed

it’s sorta like: structural progression happens when 2 things are true:

2. your environment sufficiently challenges you

you can get dysregulated at any structure. even well-developed ppl (Perverts/Seminals) haven’t necessarily learned “healthy” regulation strategies. Perverts can be quite prone to drug abuse or high-risk activities in fact.

but your structure’s mechanisms are what you “fall back” to when dysregulated. Detachers will split things from awareness, Psychotics will foreclose things (like how a Narcissist disowns things that conflict with their self-image), Neurotics will repress, Perverts will disavow.

you want to progress in structure because the more advanced mechanisms are more adaptive, better suited to dealing with life/society/world.

but to progress, you don’t want to be actively relying on your structure, you want to be *even more* regulated than that.

most people get “stuck” at their structure bc they haven’t learned to self-regulate. they just rely on their structure’s mechanism and their behaviour reflects that, their symptoms manifest (Detached derealization, Psychotic depression, Neurotic anxiety, Perverse jouissance)

more about this:

last wednesday in a convo with a friend there was a moment when i involuntarily shifted into a mode i might describe as embodied/grounded/flowing/present.

as if my centre of self was previously diffuse or projected outwards, and i brought it into my body. https://t.co/1GDfF0bbEY

suddenly i didn't feel averse to eye contact, but it actually felt good to make eye contact. like i could feel that there was myself and there was another person, separate, but linked.

whereas before it felt like we were part of a unified field, mutual extensions of each other.

what happened was we were talking (about idk what), and i essentially ran out of things to say; i ran out of cognitive energy to process what was said.

i felt panic: the same panic that i imagine some ppl feel when there's an awkward silence.

but in that moment, i made a choice. instead of reacting by changing the topic or saying "can you repeat that" or otherwise diverting attention at the cognitive level, i chose to drop into a 'noticing sensations' mode.

"i'm noticing i'm feeling warm and uncomfortable"

and then my convo partner, instead of reacting by trying to rescue my discomfort ("do you need help?" or w/e), mirrored the energy by noticing how _he_ felt: "oh, now i feel goosebumps" or smth.
it's almost like my bodymind was able to relax into safety after realizing that i'm okay, and they're okay. the discomfort is just a feeling, doesn't need to be "rescued", and went away on its own.
after that, the mode switch was complete and i felt grounded/centred for the rest of the evening and a couple days afterwards.

now it feels like that mode is gone again, but i really wanna get it back.

grateful to have learned mindfulness & authentic relating skills because i think that is what enabled me to make that choice in the moment.
